text: The surgeon marks the hernia site and identifies important surgical landmarks on the patient's abdomen in preparation for the laparoscopic hernia repair.
The surgeon transects the external oblique aponeurosis, resulting in the medialization of the rectus abdominis muscles.
The surgeon digitally develops the intermuscular plane over the ribs, preparing the area for subsequent muscular transection.
The surgeon places a dilating balloon trocar to further expand the intermuscular plane, enhancing the separation between the external oblique and internal oblique muscles.
The surgeon transsects the aponeurosis laterally to the semilunar line to avoid creating a full-thickness defect in the abdominal wall.
The inguinal ligament is visible, forming the most caudal border of the external oblique aponeurosis.
The surgeon incises and transects the external oblique aponeurosis downwards towards the inguinal ligament, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon transects the Scarpa's fascia to achieve maximum mobilization, enhancing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon places a 5mm port cranially to the inguinal ligament after sufficiently developing the surgical plane.
The surgeon uses a ligature to transect the external oblique muscle, facilitating cranial mobilization during the hernia procedure.
The surgeon further develops the precostal plane before transecting the muscle, continuing the dissection process.
The video briefly shows the right-sided component separation technique, similar to the previously demonstrated steps.
After completing the component separation, the surgeon achieves pneumoperitoneum and performs adhesiolysis to free the abdomen of adhesions. The hernia is then approximated using mattress sutures.
The surgeon places a 20x30 cm Symbiotex mesh intra-abdominally to augment the hernia repair. The mesh is attached to the ventral abdominal wall using Absorbatex fixation devices.
